description: Figure 1 displays the regression result for ferrite apparent density, defined as the inverse of the regression coefficient r_i with 95% probability error bars, compared against pure elemental densities and literature average values for alloying elements in ferritic and low-alloy steels. The horizontal bars represent the regression apparent densities, open squares denote pure elemental densities, and open triangles indicate literature averages. Data are shown for Fe, Si, Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, V, Cu, and S. The regression model (R² = 0.994) demonstrates that Cu and Mo increase ferrite density, while C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, and V reduce it. Good agreement exists between regression and literature values for Cr, Cu, Fe, Mn, Ni, S, and Si, with some deviation for Mo and notable deviation for V. Elemental densities align with regression results for Cr, Cu, Fe, Mn, Mo, and S, but deviations occur for Ni, Si, and V, indicating non-ideal solution effects. This validates the regression approach for ferrite density prediction.

This is Figure 2, a scatter plot presenting the density of cementite (structures Pnma and Pbnm) as a function of Cr and Mn mass content. Two data series are shown: Cr-bearing cementite (filled diamonds) and Mn-bearing cementite (open triangles). The vertical axis records density in Kg/m³ from 7000 to 7800, while the horizontal axis gives mass content from 0 to 1. The plot demonstrates that increasing either Cr or Mn content reduces cementite density. The Mn series spans a broad composition range, falling from approximately 7700 Kg/m³ at low content to about 7080 Kg/m³ near 0.95 mass content. The Cr series is limited to lower contents (below ~0.25), decreasing from ~7750 Kg/m³ to ~7580 Kg/m³. These measurements provide the basis for the cementite density correlations—equation (6) for Cr and equation (7) for Mn—used in the regression Model II for ferritic steels, establishing that Cr and Mn substitution significantly lowers cementite density and must be incorporated into phase-mixture density predictions.

Figure 3 presents a comparative scatter plot of apparent densities for austenite in austenitic steels derived from regression analysis, plotted against elemental densities and literature averages. Solid squares denote regression-derived apparent densities calculated as the inverse of the regression coefficient 1/r_i, open squares indicate elemental densities, and open triangles represent literature averages of apparent density, with error bars indicating uncertainty ranges. The dataset encompasses 64 batches of steel and includes alloying constituents such as Fe(fcc), Cr, Cu, Si, Co, Ni, Mn, Mo, TiC, Ti, C, and N. Key trends reveal that TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, and Ti reduce austenite density, whereas Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni increase it. Notable deviations between apparent and elemental densities occur for elements such as Si and N, attributable to bonding characteristics and structural differences. This figure supports the conclusion that the regression model achieves an R² of 0.975 with an average deviation of 2.6 kg/m³, reliably quantifying compositional effects on austenite density.
